coredata.dc:description: The outstanding properties of carbon dots (CDs), including biocompatibility, low toxicity, photoluminescence, and easy synthesis and modification, make them strong competitors to traditional semiconductor quantum dots. Chirality exists widely in nature and plays important roles in life sciences and materials sciences. The application of chirality to CDs generates a new class of potentially valuable CDs materials, chiral CDs, that may be useful in areas such as enantiomeric recognition and separation, chiral catalysis, bioimaging, and biomedicine. In the strict sense, chiral CDs synthesized by a “bottom-up” method should be classified as carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), and we therefore propose that such chiral CDs be termed chiral CPDs (Ch-CPDs). Here, we provide the first comprehensive review of recent achievements in Ch-CPDs synthesis methodology, properties, and applications, and discuss the challenges to be overcome in their future development.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The outstanding properties of carbon dots (CDs), including biocompatibility, low toxicity, photoluminescence, and easy synthesis and modification, make them strong competitors to traditional semiconductor quantum dots. Chirality exists widely in nature and plays important roles in life sciences and materials sciences. The application of chirality to CDs generates a new class of potentially valuable CDs materials, chiral CDs, that may be useful in areas such as enantiomeric recognition and separation, chiral catalysis, bioimaging, and biomedicine. In the strict sense, chiral CDs synthesized by a “bottom-up” method should be classified as carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), and we therefore propose that such chiral CDs be termed chiral CPDs (Ch-CPDs). Here, we provide the first comprehensive review of recent achievements in Ch-CPDs synthesis methodology, properties, and applications, and discuss the challenges to be overcome in their future development.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is one of the most remarkable characteristics of nature, and is important in areas of chemistry, physics, biology and medicine [1–3]. Traditional chiral organic dye molecules are complicated to synthesize, expensive, prone to photobleaching, and have poor light stability. Chiral nanostructures have become a key research field since Whetten and Schaaff (2000) discovered strong optical activity in glutathione nanoclusters, thus identifying that chirality exists at the nanoscale [4]. Much subsequent experimental and theoretical work has examined chiral nanostructures, with particular attention being paid to chiral metal nanoparticles and traditional chiral semiconductor nanostructures, which have good optical properties such as photobleaching resistance, good stability, and adjustable emission wavelength. [5–9]. However, the high cost and toxicity of these materials may hinder their application in some fields [10,11].

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as ideal substitutes for traditional semiconductor quantum dots [12]. This new class of zero-dimensional carbon-based materials has fluorescence properties and sizes of <10 nm diameter. They combine the optical properties of quantum dots with the electronic properties of carbon materials [13–16], and thus exhibit many unique advantages such as colorful photoluminescence, resistance to photobleaching, chemical stability, excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, and low toxicity [17–20]. Chirality can affect the potential applications of a material, and introducing chirality to CDs would broaden their applicability to chiral recognition [21], chiral separation [22], asymmetric catalysis [23,24] and chiral detection [25]. Suzuki et al. (2016) first reported the synthesis of chiral graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through a two-step method involving an initial “top-down” synthesis of GQDs and subsequent covalent attachment of a cysteine enantiomer [26]; however, the process is relatively complicated. Obtaining chiral CDs in one step, without secondary functionalization, capping, or doping with other enantiomers, is therefore an important new challenge. “Bottom-up” methods can meet the above requirements very well, and various methods based on it have been developed. They generally employ small enantiomeric molecules as chiral sources, chiral inheritance, or chiral induction from enantiomers to form chiral CDs [6]. Previous studies have shown that the vast majority of CDs synthesized by a “bottom-up” method should strictly be considered as carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [27]. Therefore, chiral CDs obtained by such route are here termed chiral CPDs (Ch-CPDs). Chiral carbon materials are receiving increasing attention, and chiral modifications of carbon nanotubes and fullerenes have been extensively studied [28–30]. Various reviews of CPDs have focused on their synthesis, raw materials, properties, energy conversion, photocatalytic ability, and biomedical applications [31–34], while paying little attention to their chirality. Therefore, a review of the wide variety of papers and abundant information on Ch-CPDs would aid research in this area and stimulate the development of practical applications. Here, we summarize the latest progress on Ch-CPDs by reporting on common preparation strategies, their unique properties, especially chiroptical properties, and their applications in various fields (Scheme 1 ). The final section outlines the prospects and challenges of research into Ch-CPDs.

There are two broad and comprehensive methodologies for preparing CDs: “top-down” cutting and “bottom-up” synthesis. In general, “top-down” routes lead to more regular morphologies, while “bottom-up” methods can yield a wider range of properties. Chiral CDs have been developed by both ways. “Top-down” preparation mainly involves cutting graphite-based materials with perfect sp2 carbon structures, such as carbon rods, carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes, graphite powder, carbon black, and even candle soot, via a process incorporating oxidation and exfoliation [27]. Subsequent modification of the chiral ligands can endow the CDs with chirality. Vázquez-Nakagawa et al. prepared chiral CDs by acidic exfoliation and oxidation of graphite, with subsequent esterification using enantiomerically pure (R) or (S)-2-phenyl-1-propanol [35]. However, these processes are relatively complicated, the synthesis efficiency is low (i.e., most of the products are mixtures), and it is not easy to conduct directional synthesis of chiral CDs. Therefore, research on chiral CDs based on “top-down” methods has been slow to develop, and there are few relevant reports. The “bottom-up” methods focus on the cross-linking reactions of molecules or polymers rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, or other active groups, and involve polymerization and carbonization playing vital roles in the formation of particles [36]. Therefore, the resulting CDs prepared based on this method are more accurately classified as CPDs [27]. “Bottom-up”-synthesized Ch-CPDs can simply employ chiral molecules as chiral sources. The chiral molecules can even simultaneously assume the role of carbon source in a convenient synthesis involving common hydrothermal, pyrolysis, or microwave methods. “Bottom-up” methods allow for good control of the size and shape of the CPDs, as well as heteroatom doping, thereby giving Ch-CPDs ideal performance and wide applicability. They also have clear advantages in terms of sustainability and reduced environmental impact. Consequently, Ch-CPDs have attracted great research interest. Despite initially lagging behind chiral CDs based on “top-down” methods, the field has grown rapidly and achieved outstanding results.

The use of stereospecific chiral stabilizing molecules opens up promising new methodologies in the area of nanostructure research [3]. Regarding the origin of chiral semiconductor quantum dots, the ﬁrst report was the preparation of chiral CdS [37]. The method involved mixing chiral penicillamine and cadmium perchlorate, then adding thioacetamide to produce CdS nanoclusters, and finally annealing to obtain highly luminescent chiral CdS quantum dots. Subsequently, similar techniques were used to prepare Ch-CPDs [38]. Given the potential applicability of optoelectronic chiral materials, strategies for the fabrication of Ch-CPDs are important to broaden the application of CPDs materials. There are currently three major synthesis strategies for Ch-CPDs: two-step synthesis [39], one-step synthesis [40], and chiral assembly [41]. CPDs can inherit the chiral properties of the precursor molecules, thereby allowing for a simple strategy for synthesizing chiral nanomaterials. Most methods for preparing Ch-CPDs employ amino acid enantiomers as chiral sources, including lysine, cysteine, penicillamine, glutamic acid, aspartic acid, and tryptophan. Some use other enantiomers, such as cyclohexanediamine. Chiral assembly involves constructing Ch-CPDs using cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) and a chiral gelator.

Two-step synthesis, which was the first method used to prepare Ch-CPDs, is based on the chiral modification of achiral carbon dots. The first step is generally carbonization to form a highly carbonized carbon skeleton with reactive groups. As most carbon sources are rich in carboxyl groups, such groups are the most popular reactive sites on the surface of the carbon skeleton. However, excessive carboxyl groups on the surface of CPDs often lead to recombination of electron–hole pairs and emissive traps, which may affect the fluorescence properties of the CPDs [42]. In addition to the second step of modifying the chiral ligand performing directional function design (to enable the Ch-CPDs to perform specific tasks), the chiral ligand can be used as a stabilizer to improve the fluorescence properties of the carbon skeleton. The modification of chiral ligands is mostly based on the reaction of reactive groups on the surface of the carbon skeleton with chiral molecules via covalent bonds, electrostatic interactions, and hydrogen bonds. Among them, the covalent coupling of amide bonds is the most common modification strategy. This method of surface passivation of CPDs with chiral ligands generates surface defects, thereby enhancing photoluminescence, and also supplies potential reactive sites required for further functional modification [43]. The highly carbonized carbon skeleton usually results in the good stability of Ch-CPDs synthesized by this method. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, Li et al. [44] successfully prepared Ch-CPDs by pyrolyzing citric acid to form carbon nuclei, then modified them with l-/d-cysteine via covalent coupling of amide bond. Co-doping with nitrogen and sulfur led to strong fluorescence emission. The introduction of sulfur atoms seems to eliminate the O-state, enhance the N-state, and enhance the effect of nitrogen atoms on the performance of doped carbon nanomaterials through a synergistic effect. After the first carbonization, the fluorescence quantum yield was only 4.1 %, but increased to 68 % after chiral ligand modification. More importantly, the free thiol group brought by the cysteine ligand gives the Ch-CPDs a distinctive capacity for long-term in situ Golgi imaging. Due to the presence of a highly carbonized carbon skeleton means that the Ch-CPDs show good photostability even under continuous laser excitation for 3600 s. Yuan et al. [45] pyrolyzed the same citric acid carbon source and used l-/d-penicillamine as a chiral source. During synthesis, the primary formed CPDs had a low fluorescence quantum yield of 4.8 %, and acted as the carbon core for the subsequent formation of chiral luminescent CPDs. Through the formation of amide bonds in the second carbonization process, the optically active penicillamine molecules could be attached to the surface of primary CPDs, increasing the fluorescence quantum yield to 50 %. The Ch-CPDs exhibited good light stability, ionic strength stability, pH stability, and antioxidant capacity. Ostadhossein et al. [46] prepared negatively charged CPDs by microwave carbonization of sucrose, and their surfaces contained many hydrophilic functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxylate, and esters). Ch-CPDs were prepared by introducing various chiral amino acids on the surfaces of CPDs via 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl) carbodiimide/ N-hydroxy succinimide conjugation at room temperature. Note that the reaction temperature for the second carbonization must be lower than the melting point of the enantiomers used for surface passivation in order to generate chiral centers and maintain chirality. Rao et al. [39] pyrolyzed tartaric acid as a carbon skeleton, and then modified it with l-/d-penicillamine to prepare Ch-CPDs. They found that conducting the secondary carbonization at 206 °C, above the melting point of penicillamine, led to achiral products.

Two-step syntheses first gave chiral characteristics to CPDs, but with the drawbacks of complicated and long processing, harsh conditions, and high cost. Consequently, subsequent efforts were focused on developing a simpler one-step method.

Compared with two-step synthesis, one-step synthesis is more convenient and can directly process chiral molecules or a mixture containing a carbon and chiral sources to produce Ch-CPDs. The reaction may proceed through condensation between chiral precursors or between the chiral precursor and the carbon source, and then by aggregation to form larger nanoparticles, which are eventually carbonized to form small Ch-CPDs [27]. By controlling the reaction conditions to control the degree of carbonization, the Ch-CPDs can retain a “chiral structural memory” of the chiral compounds employed as building blocks. This method avoids the synthesis of achiral carbon nuclei and subsequent coupling with chiral molecules, so that carbon nanomaterials and chiroptical properties are fully combined. The first reported one-step preparation of Ch-CPDs by Malishev et al. [47] used lysine as a carbon and chiral source to produce Ch-CPDs by hydrothermal reaction at a relatively low temperature of 170℃. Hu et al. [48] successfully prepared Ch-CPDs co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur by hydrothermal treatment of cysteine at 120℃. Fig. 1b shows the method developed by Li et al. [49] for preparing Ch-CPDs co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur by hydrothermal treatment of cysteine at temperatures as low as 60 °C, with fluorescence quantum yields of 41 %. The results showed that cysteine is unstable in aqueous solutions, and thus appears unpromising for high-temperature preparation of nanomaterials utilizing it as a stabilizer. Arshad et al. [50] carried out one-step microwave treatment of an ethanol solution of para-benzoquinone and l-/d-cysteine to synthesize Ch-CPDs co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur. The whole process took only 10 min, and resulted in a maximum fluorescence quantum yield of 36 % in ethanol solution. One-step synthesis usually employs a lower carbonization temperature to maintain the existence of chirality, and so cannot form a carbon core with a high degree of carbonization. Therefore, the Ch-CPDs prepared by this method are slightly less stable than CPDs with a high carbonization degree, but they still appear promising and have widespread practical applicability. Gao et al. [51] explored the stability of Ch-CPDs obtained by a one-step method under different conditions: they showed good stability with respect to ionic strength, light, temperature, and pH. This was because the Ch-CPDs had the chiral molecules confined in the skeleton of a polymer/carbon hybrid by covalent bonds, space-limited interactions, and supramolecular interactions: as a molecular state related structure, the fluorescence was thus truly from the nanoparticles of the CPDs rather than from unbonded organic fluorescent molecules, which allowed the Ch-CPDs to maintain fairly good stability [52]. Zhang et al. [53] prepared Ch-CPDs based on glutamic acid by means of electrochemical polymerization. Their chirality can be adjusted or even reversed by controlling the reaction time, in the first demonstration of reversible chirality in CPDs. In most cases, the chirality of composites composed of CPDs and CNCs is determined by the geometrical arrangement of CPDs on CNCs surfaces [54]. However, Chekini et al. [55] achieved direct and scalable hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped Ch-CPDs by using CNCs as a chiral substrate and carbon source under reflux conditions. Unlike most works, chirality was not derived from the geometrical arrangement of CPDs on the CNCs surfaces, but from the retention of chiral glucose molecules on the surfaces under mild preparation conditions. The glucose moieties of the CNCs surfaces reacted with ethylenediamine, and formed CPDs under reflux conditions. In addition to being a nitrogen source for CPDs doping, ethylenediamine facilitated the condensation/polymerization reaction. Ghosh et al. [56] discovered particularly interesting non-graphite nuclear CPDs. Guanosine 5′-monophosphate is a self-assembling natural nucleotide with unique potential to form ordered supramolecular structures, and its phosphate group can catalyze the formation of CPDs and confer water solubility. Its sodium salt was used to form blue Ch-CPDs with up- and down-conversion fluorescence, which can self-assemble to form a fluorescent hydrogel.

Compared with the two-step synthesis, the one-step method is simple, convenient, and efficient, requiring no secondary modification. Its strong advantages make the potential applications of Ch-CPDs more attainable.

Another strategy to endow CPDs with chirality is chiral assembly. This involves arranging CPDs according to the chiral structure or shape of the chiral matrix, thereby transferring the chirality of the matrix to the CPDs [41,57] CNCs are the most hopeful templates for the synthesis of photonic nanomaterials. Their unique self-assembly features and biocompatibility provide excellent properties for CPDs [58]. Fig. 1c illustrates the synthesis of nitrogen-doped CPDs by Lizundia et al. [59] using melamine and d-glucose, and assembly with CNCs. They demonstrated, for the first time, a method of preparing independent, iridescent, and luminescent chiral nematic N-CPDs/CNCs composite materials after N-CPDs co-assembly with CNCs via evaporation-induced self-assembly. Zheng et al. [54] selected typical methods for the ultrasonic processing of a suspension of CNCs with added CPDs to form chiral nematic sequences during subsequent evaporation. By adding blue-, green-, and red-emitting CPDs, nanomaterials with circularly polarized luminescence are obtained. In addition to CNCs, chiral gelators are also ideal chiral substrates. Zhou et al. [60] doped Ch-CPDs into a matching N,N′-bis(octadecyl)-d-aminoglutamic diamide (DGAm) chiral gelator system to achieve the superposition of chiroptical signals and the combination of optics.
Chiral assembly not only imparts chirality to CPDs, but also provides an organic substrate, making CPDs promising materials for advanced optical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and optical properties of Ch-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ch-CPDs should be chiral molecular fluorophores attaching to a carbon core [61]. At this stage, the carbon core can be divided into two types of completely carbonized cores similar to carbon quantum dots or carbon nanodots [52]. Ch-CPDs are more stable than their precursors due to the carbonization, and show better compatibility than quantum dots due to the preserved molecular fluorophores [27]. Their many special optoelectronic properties largely determine their applications. The electronic properties are useful in energy storage [62] and chiral catalysis [31], and their optical properties are biomedically useful [63].

Chiral sources are usually amino acid enantiomers [48,49,53] so the Ch-CPDs are composed mainly of C, N, O, and S. Their nitrogen and sulfur co-doping causes the p orbitals of the carbon atoms to be conjugated with the lone-pair electrons of the heteroatoms, so that the Ch-CPDs have special optical and electronic properties [64]. They are usually crystalline or amorphous, with various surface functional groups. Their abundant oxygen-containing functional groups give them excellent water solubility and provide opportunity for further surface functionalization.

Various analytical methods are commonly applied to characterize Ch-CPDs to determine the crystal structure of the carbon atoms, the type of surface functional groups, and the elements contained. Such investigations can improve understanding of the unique physical properties of Ch-CPDs. Their particle sizes are generally <10 nm diameter. Fig. 1d shows a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of Ch-CPDs [48], indicating good dispersion. Further observation by high-resolution TEM provides information about the lattice fringe spacing: Fig. 1e shows lattice spacings of 0.21 and 0.32 nm, corresponding to graphene’s in-plane lattice spacing (100 facet) and inter-layer (002 facet), respectively. Furthermore, Fig. 1f shows the TEM image and selected area (electron) diﬀ ;raction pattern of amorphous Ch-CPDs [65]. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) is often used to measure the height of Ch-CPDs, and Fig. 1g shows a height of 4–5 nm [48]. The crystallinity of the prepared Ch-CPDs is also often analyzed using X-ray diffractometry (XRD): the typical spectrum in Fig. 1h shows a broad diffraction peak with a 2Θ value of 20°–30°, which is attributed to disordered carbon atoms [65].

The surface groups of Ch-CPDs are usually determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), which can also characterize changes in the reaction process and the successful connection between the carbon skeleton and chiral molecules. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) has also been used to analyze the elemental composition and chemical status of Ch-CPDs. Fig. 1i illustrates FTIR the results characterizing the carbon skeleton, cysteine, and Ch-CPDs [66]. In contrast to the carbon skeleton, the Ch-CPDs exhibit a peak at 1700 cm−1, which may be attributed to the interaction between the carbon skeleton and cysteine. The peak at 1620 cm−1 is attributed to the NH, inherited from the amino group in cysteine. The other strong peak at 3456 cm−1 is the OH vibration from the cysteine and carbon skeleton. The analysis shows the successful preparation of Ch-CPDs through linking of cysteine to the carbon skeleton. The surface composition of Ch-CPDs measured by XPS is consistent with the FTIR results. The full-scan XPS spectrum of Ch-CPDs in Fig. 1j shows the presence of C, O, N, and S elements without impurities.

Thermogravimetric analysis measures mass loss with increasing temperature [67]. It determines the thermostability of Ch-CPDs and also identifies and quantifies fluorophores. As molecular ﬂuorophores are thermally less stable than the carbon core—they lose mass at up to 300 °C, while the carbon core loses no mass until around 800 °C—they will be damaged (without disturbing the core) at their melting temperature, and the total mass loss of the sample divided by the mass of the ﬂuorophore indicates the number of ﬂuorophores attached to the core in Ch-CPDs [61].

Unlike conventional chiral semiconductor quantum dots, neither the optical absorption nor the fluorescence emission of Ch-CPDs is caused by the band gap, but by radiative recombination and π-plasmons of the holes and surface-confined electrons [68].

Ordinarily, the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum of Ch-CPDs has two well-defined absorption peaks in the ultraviolet region (Fig. 2 a). The high-energy peak is usually due to aromatic sp2 domain π–π* transitions [69]. Compared with chiral ligands, due to the extended conjugated domain, the high-energy peak of Ch-CPDs usually moves to a higher wavenumber. The low-energy peak is assigned to n–π* transitions of CO, −OH, –NH2, and –SH bonds [31]. To maintain the existence of chiral molecular fluorophores on the surface of CPDs, Ch-CPDs usually have a low degree of carbonization or are modified by chiral ligands. Therefore, the functional groups attached on their surfaces include many structural configurations that can increase the amount of electronic structures with different energy levels. This enhances the possibilities for the recombination of electrons and holes captured by the surface states. The π-electron system in the carbon cores can couple with these functional groups, synergistically changing the electronic structures of the CPDs and their recombination properties [70]. Compared with CPDs with a high degree of carbonization, the absorbance of most Ch-CPDs is blue-shifted. Ostadhossein et al. [46] modified the synthesized achiral CPDs with chiral ligands, and the absorption of the resulting Ch-CPDs was blue-shifted, which was possibly due to substitution of the surface groups with electron withdrawing groups because of conjugation (Fig. 2b). In addition, the defect centers of Ch-CPDs can easily interact with the surrounding environment, thereby altering the electronic structures of Ch-CPDs, leading to obvious optical changes. For example, some Ch-CPDs have exhibited pH-dependent UV–vis absorption (Fig. 2c), and surface chemistry plays a major role in such cases [49].

Many papers have focused on Ch-CPDs photoluminescence (PL) characteristics. Their fluorescence mechanisms are similar to those of CPDs, with possible mechanisms involving the effects of surface state, subdomain state, molecular state, and crosslink-enhanced emission [27,52]. Fig. 2d shows the excitation dependence of results of Rao et al. [39] who made Ch-CPDs using tartaric acid and l-/d-penicidamine. Various surface functional groups can cause a series of emissive traps between π and π* states in Ch-CPDs, and changing the excitation wavelength causes different surface energy traps to dominate the emission. Therefore, the distributions of various surface energy traps and particle sizes could reasonably account for the observed excitation-dependent PL [71]. Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped Ch-CPDs hydrothermally synthesized from cysteine by Li et al. [49] showed reversible pH-dependent PL emission intensity (Fig. 2e), but the emission was not obviously excitation dependent. Red-shifting the excitation wavelength slightly red-shifted the PL emission wavelength (Fig. 2f), possibly due to surface chemistry effects. The numerous amino and carbonyl groups on the surfaces of the Ch-CPDs, along with nitrogen and sulfur atoms inside them, generate surface energy traps [72,73] with the surface defects causing bright emission. The reversible pH-dependent PL emission is attributed to the contribution of carbonyl- and amino-based local electronic states. Wei et al. [40] used tryptophan to prepare Ch-CPDs that exhibited excitation-independent emissions (Fig. 2g), which they attributed to either closely similar or identical emissive sites. Combining Ch-CPDs with other materials to create composites also affects their PL properties. Zhou et al. [60] combined glutamic acid Ch-CPDs with a common gelator, DGAm. The resulting composite not only retained the fluorescence characteristics of the Ch-CPDs, but had a fluorescence efficiency improved by 50 % (Fig. 2h and i); the quantum yield also increased from 20.9% to 32.5%. CPDs usually undergo random Brownian motion in aqueous solution [74]; their collisions with each other will cause partial fluorescence quenching. However, adding CPDs to a gel system more or less fixes their position, thereby reducing collision quenching and significantly improving the practical applicability of Ch-CPDs.

Carbonization is important in the formation of CPDs and is a key influence on their properties. The carbonization degree usually increases with increasing temperature of thermal treatment, and the molecular state gradually changes to the carbon core state, resulting in a larger size and more effective conjugation structure with red-shifted emission [52]. Compared with highly carbonized CPDs, Ch-CPDs are usually made with a lower carbonization temperature in order to maintain the chiral center, resulting in the advantage of molecular photoluminescence. The lower temperature also leads to poorer graphitization, which results in a smaller conjugated structure that includes subdomain with smaller size and molecular state–related structure with a smaller degree of conjugation. Therefore, compared with highly carbonized CPDs, Ch-CPDs usually show blue-shifted emission. Copur et al. [75] found that after modifying the carbon skeleton with chiral ligands, the emission peak of the CPDs was blue-shifted by about 20 nm. Ostadhossein et al. [46] also found that modification with chiral ligands shifted the emission peak from 489 to 486 nm. Unfortunately, insufﬁcient puriﬁcation and incomplete characterization might obfuscate the correct attribution of PL properties of “bottom-up” CPDs [76]. Recent reports suggest that ﬂuorescence associated with CPDs may originate notably from molecular ﬂuorophores and/or their aggregates or polymer dots. These may be produced as by-products or even as the sole product of CPDs synthesis [77,78]. During the preparation of Ch-CPDs, the lower carbonization temperature required to generate chiral centers and maintain chirality means that the above phenomenon of incorrect attribution of PL characteristics is more common. However, this is often overlooked in reports of Ch-CPDs. Fortunately, the above-mentioned phenomenon in Ch-CPDs have a limited effect on their performance and have not affected their widespread practical applicability.

The chiroptical properties of Ch-CPDs create their most intriguing features, and are usually characterized by circular dichroism (CD) spectra. While l-CPDs and d-CPDs have the same UV–vis absorption, their opposing left- and right-polarized light absorption demonstrate that they are enantiomers [37]. CD depends on the difference in the absorption of left and right circularly polarized radiation by chromophores with inherent chirality or placed in a chiral environment [79]. Therefore, we simply divide the CD signals of Ch-CPDs into two parts: one inherited from the chiral ligand or chiral matrix, while the remaining signals are attributed to the chiral environment [80]. Few previous studies have examined the true mechanism of chirality in Ch-CPDs, which is usually explained by the origin of chiroptical activity in chiral-monolayer-protected metal clusters [7,39].

Hu et al. [48] prepared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped Ch-CPDs from cysteine. Fig. 3 a shows a CD signal at 212 nm derived from l-/d-cysteine, another signal at 240 nm is generated by the π–π* conjugation of the sp2-hybridized carbon network in the Ch-CPDs [81]. Wei et al. [40] made Ch-CPDs using tryptophan, and elucidated the origin of their (Fig. 3b). Similarly, the chiral chromophore inherited from tryptophan generates a CD signal near 220 nm, and the signal at 240 nm can be assigned to π–π* conjugation of the sp2-hybridized carbon network. There is also an additional signal at 290 nm. The chiral origin was explored by designing three parallel groups of CPDs using raw materials of indole, alanine, and both together. The indole CPDs were achiral, while those made with chiral alanine were chiral (around 205 nm). Using indole and alanine together led to a new chiral signal at 375 nm, because of the distortion of the achiral chromophores [80] and the generation of chirality in a chiral environment. It was further proved that the CD signal at 290 nm in CPDs made using tryptophan was induced by the chiral environment. Rao et al. [39] prepared Ch-CPDs by a two-step method, and revealed that the achiral CPDs were transformed into Ch-CPDs by surface passivation with chiral ligands. The carbon source made use of achiral citric acid, while l-/d-pen-CA [pen = penicillamine; CA = citric acid] showed clear CD signals at 206, 225, 247, and 337 nm (Fig. 3c). The signal at 225 nm came from the ligand l-/d-penicillamine, the other signals were attributed to transitions between different quantum levels, and the quantum transitions were derived from forbidden band transitions of the conjugate sp2 domain [82]. As Fig. 3d shows, the origin of chiroptical properties can be explained with the help of a vicinal-effect model. During passivation of the CPDs surfaces using the l-/d-penicillamine ligand, the asymmetric electric field of the chiral ligand can break the symmetry of the electron state of the carbon nucleus, forming CPDs with chiroptical property [82]. Rao et al. also pointed out that the pyrolysis temperature of the second step was lower than the melting point of the chiral ligand (Fig. 3e), which is essential to obtain the required Ch-CPDs, probably arising from a chiral induction [83] or chiral imprint [84]. Ðorđević et al. [85] gave CPDs chirality through a hydrothermal bottom-up microwave assisted synthesis using trans-cyclohexanediamine and arginine. The chirality was attributed to the existence of many cyclohexanediamine surrounding a carbon-based amorphous core. The vibration CD spectra in Fig. 3f show the chirality of the CPDs: strong bands are concentrated around 1600 cm−1. Simulated spectra for the optimized structures attribute strong signals to cyclohexane CH and primary amine N–H bending vibrational modes; the band at approximately 1600 cm−1 has contributions from the CO functional groups of amide. Zhou et al. [60] successfully doped Ch-CPDs into a gel system without destroying the structure of the gel. Fig. 3g shows that CPDs–DGAm exhibited an augmented positive Cotton effect at 230 nm, and a superimposed CD signal at 210 nm. This indicates that Ch-CPDs can be incorporated into a gel system to achieve chiral adjustment of the gel, while retaining the original chiral characteristics of the Ch-CPDs.

In general, the optical rotations of Ch-CPDs are consistent with those of chiral ligands. Zhang et al. [53] discovered Ch-CPDs with optical rotations opposite to that of the precursor. The optical activity of the Ch-CPDs was easily regulated by controlling the reaction time. Fig. 3h shows the CD signals of Ch-CPDs obtained with diverse electrolysis times were monitored. After one day of electrolysis, the signal was at 205 nm and originated from the glutamate precursor. Electrolysis for two days significantly increased signal intensity, but it decreased after three days of electrolysis. Electrolysis for four days extinguished the signal. Unexpectedly, even longer electrolysis induced the reverse signal at the same location, and its intensity gradually increased. This discovery breaks the restriction that Ch-CPDs with targeted optical activity must be obtained from precursors with corresponding optical rotation. In rare cases, the CD spectra of Ch-CPDs do not exhibit enantiomeric behavior. Deka et al. [65] hydrothermally synthesized Ch-CPDs whose CD spectra were red-shifted (Fig. 3i), possibly because biomolecules exhibit circular differential scattering [86] and absorption flattening [87]. Furthermore, some researchers have suggested that solvents can also affect CD spectra [88]. Ch-CPDs obtained via hydrothermal route, this impacts the enantiomeric form of the CPDs. However, their CD spectra and specific rotation values confirm the retention of chirality.
Chiroptical properties are the most attractive features of Ch-CPDs as combining chirality with CPDs has important potential applications in many fields.

After being endowed with chirality, CPDs show many exciting applications [89]. Moreover, Ch-CPDs are often used in composite materials, which is a significant way of expanding their applications. Various groups have reported applications related to Ch-CPDs. This section summarizes the use of Ch-CPDs in sensing, biology, medicine, catalysis, and some other fields.

Ch-CPDs can be used for the recognition and separation of enantiomers. Copur et al. [75] prepared l-cysteine-modified Ch-CPDs by two-step method. They were used to identify chiral lysine. Among various amino acid enantiomers, the PL sensor showed only an enantioselective reaction to l-lysine due to its conformational difference. The study also proposed combining Ch-CPDs and nanopaper, which would open a new way to develop innovative enantiosensing strategies (Fig. 4 a). Vulugundam et al. [90] prepared Ch-CPDs by surface passivation with chiral aspartic acid, which were employed as chiral adsorbents for enantiospecific linkage and a platform for enantioselective separation of racemic mixtures. Subsequently, Gao et al. [51] developed a more facile one-step route to synthesize Ch-CPDs from citric acid and l-aspartic acid. The fluorescence of Ch-CPDs is quenched by Sn2+ and restored by l-lysine. Based on the two processes of On–Off and Off–On fluorescence, Ch-CPDs have been utilized in a simple and efficient dual-sensor for intracellular Sn2+ detection and chiral recognition of lysine (Fig. 4b). Liu et al. [91] attempted to connect Ch-CPDs with a metal–organic framework (MOF) to establish an efficient electrochemical sensing platform. The resulting composite material had Ch-CPDs serving as a perfect electroactive substance for enantiospecific determination, while the MOF provided a compatible porous support matrix. The synergy of Ch-CPDs and the porous framework enhanced electron transport, and also provided an electron-transfer channel between the medium and the surface of the electrode. Therefore, Ch-CPDs@MOF/CuF showed high sensitivity in detecting the enantiomers of penicillamine (Fig. 4c). Hou et al. [92] reported the electrodeposition of Ch-CPDs and MOF on Ni foil to construct a MOF@CPDs/NiF electrode, which showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity to tyrosine enantiomers. It also has the ability to determine the proportion of l-tyrosine in a mixture of tyrosine enantiomers. Huo et al. [93] successfully prepared a new chiral fluorescent hybridization probe CPDs@Eu(D-cam) [D-cam = (1R,3S)-(+)-Camphoric acid] by encapsulating CPDs into a chiral Eu(D-cam) coordination framework. The resulting probe was applied in the targeted detection of l-phenylalanine (Fig. 4d), and could be further developed for the discrimination of l-phenylalanine in urine specimens. Pandey et al. [94] developed electropolymerized, molecularly imprinted, polyaniline ferrocenesulfonic acid-CPDs modified, pencil graphite electrodes, and demonstrated their use in the separation and quantification of d-/L-ascorbic acid in aqueous and biological samples (Fig. 4e). Due to the memory effect of the polymer film, the formed chiral recognition sites could distinguish target chiral molecules by their size, shape, and functional group orientation. The CPDs and polymer film provide a large electrochemically active surface and have functional groups that influence the electrostatic interactions associated with electrochemical processes such as adsorption of l-ascorbic acid in the imprinted cavity. Chiral detection of l-ascorbic acid has been successfully demonstrated in pharmaceutical and human plasma samples via the proposed sensor, which showed good selectivity and sensitivity.

Ch-CPDs can also be used for detection molecules and ions. Arshad et al. [50] used benzoquinone and l-cysteine precursors in ethanol in the microwave-assisted synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped Ch-CPDs, which showed prominent aggregation-induced emission behavior. The Ch-CPDs were applied to detect water impurities in various polar solvents (Fig. 4f), and were particularly sensitive in acetone. They could also detect and determine the presence of trace amounts of moisture in commercial products and pharmaceuticals. Importantly, they are reusable. Sun et al. [95] developed a chiral gel system doped with CPDs. The addition of CPDs made the gel fluoresce and enhanced its mechanical strength. Coating it on a silica gel plate prepared a solid sensing system for the rapid and semi-quantitative detection of aqueous Cu2+ (Fig. 4g). The co-assembly of Ch-CPDs and biocompatible surfactants represents a new probability of colloid science, supramolecular chemistry, and biology. Lizundia et al. [59] hydrothermally treated d-glucose and melamine to produce nitrogen-doped CPDs, which exhibited chirality through chiral arrangements on CNCs. The composite chiral nanomaterials possessed both luminescence and iridescence chirality, and were employed as a luminescence sensor for biological imaging or detecting metal ions.

Ch-CPDs have excellent biocompatibility, light stability, and long-term in situ imaging capabilities. The good biocompatibility of CDs forms the basis of their application in various biomedicines, and sometimes the chirality will also have a different impact on its biocompatibility. Yuan et al. [45] developed blue-emitting Ch-CPDs by the two-step pyrolysis of citric acid and penicillamine enantiomers. Although both l-CPDs and d-CPDs showed low cytotoxicity, there was chiral dependence, with the latter being less toxic. Subcellular localization examination showed that both CPDs particularly targeted and illuminated the Golgi apparatus, but there was no significant difference in their subcellular localizations. This discovery broadens the application of Ch-CPDs to subcellular imaging, and provides a new approach to tracking cell status by real-time monitoring of the morphology of the Golgi apparatus. The technique may have important implications for the design of novel drug carriers for some Golgi-related diseases. Li et al. [44] synthesized l-CPDs with an l-type spatial structure rich in cysteine residues. The free thiol groups and l-type stereo structure gave the CPDs excellent abilities for the long-term in situ targeting and imaging of Golgi apparatus (Fig. 5 a) [96,97]. Compared with l-CPDs, d-CPDs exhibit limited Golgi targeting ability, indicating that the chirality of CPDs strongly influences their ability to target the Golgi. They could track the real-time progress of intracellular damage (such as viral infection) by detecting morphological changes of the Golgi apparatus, and thus provide a new model for studying changes related to the early diagnosis of viral infection. Unlike CD, which studies the chiral structure information of the ground state, circular polarization luminescence reflects structural information of the excited state of a chiral luminescent system [98]. The technique has received much recent attention. Chekini et al. [55] exploited Ch-CPDs using CNCs as a carbon and chiral source. The hybrid CPDs/CNCs nanoparticles revealed asymmetric circularly polarized luminescence, with left-handed light being emitted more strongly than right-handed (Fig. 5b). New strategies for biological imaging have been introduced to reduce background PL and improve image quality.

Stereo interactions between cells and chiral surfaces can occur through special chemical interactions. Ostadhossein et al. [46] used various amino acids to modify the surfaces of CPDs. The Ch-CPDs modified by some cyclic α-amino acids were flipped in rotation compared with the original ligand. These Ch-CPDs were found to interact with the cellular membrane or its mimic in a highly selective manner due to their acquired asymmetric selectivity (Fig. 5c). Compared with cells treated with l-proline Ch-CPDs, those treated with d-proline Ch-CPDs had lower viability and higher rates of cell death and growth inhibition. Similar behavior was observed for treatment with d-phenylalanine Ch-CPDs. As chiral molecules are critically important in chemistry, biology, and medicine, this study will advance our understanding of how enantiomerically pure chiral nanoscale materials can be synthesized while modulating certain cellular functions. Li et al. [49] first demonstrated the differential effects of Ch-CPDs on cell energy metabolism. l-CPDs can significantly promote glycolysis in cells, while d-CPDs have no such effect. However, both have good biocompatibility that is not chiral dependent, and neither significantly affected aerobic respiration by cells (Fig. 5d). Zhang et al. [99] first demonstrated that Ch-CPDs have a systematic impact on the growth of mung bean plants: upon being absorbed, they promote growth and photosynthesis, thus improving the root and stem length of mung-bean sprouts, with d-CPDs having a more-prominent effect than l-CPDs, and they show no difference in biocompatibility at limited concentrations (Fig. 5e). This shows that Ch-CPDs can modulate plant growth, and represents a new idea of promoting plant quality. For example, they could be used as agricultural fertilizer. Ch-CPDs have opened novel ways for treating disease. Arad et al. [100] discovered that Ch-CPDs based on lysine enantiomers can be used as ﬁbrillation modulators for prion peptide (PrP, 106–126 sequence). In the presence of a lipid bilayer, the interaction between Ch-CPDs and the amyloidogenic determinant of PrP(106–126) is highly stereoselective (Fig. 5f), with l-CPDs being superior to d-CPDs in regulating the structural transformation and aggregation of PrP(106–126). This work opens a new direction for the development of Ch-CPDs as therapy for amyloidosis. Malishev et al. [47] also demonstrated that Ch-CPDs made from lysine can affect the cytotoxicity and aggregation of amyloid beta-42 (Aβ42) (Fig. 5g). l- and d-CPDs showed differing chiral-dependent cytotoxicity: the former’s was negligible, while the latter exhibited a small negative effect on cell viability. Compared with d-CPDs, l-CPDs showed a higher affinity for Aβ42 (pre-fibrillar species and/or monomeric), and can regulate its fibril assembly process, cytotoxicity, and membrane interactions. Aβ42 is the main component of the fibrillar amyloid plaques associated with Alzheimer’s disease, and its modulation through Ch-CPDs may introduce new therapeutic strategies for the disease. Xin et al. [81] demonstrated that Ch-CPDs based on d-glutamic acid could destroy both Gram-negative and Grampositive bacterial cell walls, resulting in the death of bacteria, while their counterpart l-CPDs based on l-glutamic acid had a negligible effect on bacteria. Both showed good biocompatibility with mammalian cells, suggesting that Ch-CPDs could be used as chiral antimicrobial nanoagents (Fig. 5h). This effective antimicrobial strategy provides clues to the development of novel chiral antimicrobial nanoparticles with high efficiency and excellent biosafety.

Ch-CPDs possess an excellent capacity for enantioselective electrochemical recognition of object molecules with certain configurations. Ch-CPDs prepared by Zhang et al. [66] can be employed as an excellent catalyst for the electrocatalysis of small molecules, such as l-/d-tart. Hu et al. [48] reported Ch-CPDs that can strongly adsorb small chiral molecules with the same configuration due to the influence of the spatial configuration, thereby accelerating charge transfer and enhancing catalytic ability (Fig. 6 a). Enzymes are efficient biocatalysts, but although they are specific and efficient, their catalytic activity is often difficult to regulate, and enzymes are unstable [101,102]. However, modification by Ch-CPDs can regulate their catalytic activity. The Ch-CPDs prepared by Hu et al. [103] can selectively regulate laccase activity (Fig. 6b). Their connection to laccase through non-covalent bonding forces and the interaction of chirality and nanoparticles will affect the structure of laccase. l-CPDs can increase the activity of laccase to 20 %, while d-CPDs reduce it to 10 %. This simple method provides a new approach for developing diverse Ch-CPDs nanomaterials as chiral catalysts. Zhang et al. [53] subsequently derived Ch-CPDs from glutamic acid that could significantly regulate maltase activity through covalent bonding with it (Fig. 6c), with l-CPDs and d-CPDs demonstrating different inhibition rates (15.5 % and 54.7 %, respectively). This work explored the use of Ch-CPDs as an alpha-glucosidase inhibitor to regulate blood glucose levels, and provides a new approach to antihyperglycemic remedies. Nanomaterials with enzyme-mimetic activities, termed nanozymes. Compared with natural enzymes, nanozymes exhibit better catalytic stability, longer storage times, greater ease of modification, and lower cost, making them the most promising alternative to natural enzymes [104–106]. Li et al. [107] discovered that cysteine-derived Ch-CPDs can mimic topoisomerase I to enantioselectively mediate topological rearrangement of supercoiled DNA (Fig. 6d). d-CPDs can, more effectively than l-CPDs, catalyze the topological transition of plasmid DNA from supercoiled to nicked open-circular configuration. Enzymatic enantioselectivity of Ch-CPDs and the topoisomerase I mimicking activity allow important and prospective applications such as gene manipulation and protein engineering.

In addition to achievements in the above fields, the unique performance of Ch-CPDs have drawn attention in other areas. Xiong et al. [108] exploited self-assembled emissive needle-like nanostructures through modifying CNCs with CPDs, with the CPDs forming a chiral arrangement in the CNCs matrix. This nanostructure is expected to be useful in biophoton applications such as anti-counterfeiting, waveguides, lasers, and optical vortex control (Fig. 6e). Deka et al. [65] prepared a series of Ch-CPDs. The l-/D-CPDs interacted differently with azobenzene photosensitizer molecules that had been converted from the trans to the cis isomer (Fig. 6f), showing that CPDs chirality will affect the optical properties of the photosensitizer. Ðorđević et al. [85] prepared Ch-CPDs by hydrothermal microwave-assisted synthesis. The chirality was transferred from the Ch-CPDs to tetranionic porphyrins by electrostatic interactions, which induced the formation of chiral aggregates. This discovery opens possibilities for the development of Ch-CPDs composites.
The applications of Ch-CPDs are diverse, and we hope to encounter more applications in the future, so as to make their prospects even brighter.

This review summarizes progress related to the synthesis, properties, and applications of Ch-CPDs. They are prepared mainly by one-step synthesis, which is quick and convenient. Their various compositions, structures, and physical and optical properties are discussed, as are their main applications in selective recognition, enantiomeric separation, molecular and ion detection, bioimaging, biomedicine, and chiral catalysis. The advantages of Ch-CPDs include environmental friendliness, low cost, good biocompatibility, high chemical stability, and easy functionalization, and have led to emerging research into Ch-CPDs composites, which could greatly widen the range of Ch-CPDs applicability. Chirality brings many notable characteristics from the optics to the electronics for the CPDs. At the same time, the design principle of Ch-CPDs helps to transfer chirality from the molecular level to the nanometer level. Existing research has made significant progress in the field of Ch-CPDs, but there remain some challenges.
(1)
Previous research has encountered the narrow-band chiral signal and the difficulty in implementing circularly polarised emissions as the main limiting factors for the optoelectronic applications of Ch-CPDs. Hence, an emphasis for future research should be the preparation of Ch-CPDs with visible and even near infrared (NIR) chiral signals and circularly polarized emission. An additional remaining problem is the synthesis of Ch-CPDs with long-wavelength emissions and high fluorescence quantum yield. Most reported Ch-CPDs have emissions at UV–vis wavelengths, while the preparation of CPDs with longer emission wavelengths is more complicated. In organisms, the stronger energies of relatively weakly penetrating blue and green light cause greater damage to biological tissues when compared with red or NIR light, which has little interference, strong tissue penetration, and causes little damage to organisms. Developing simple methodologies for the large-scale preparation of Ch-CPDs with good biocompatibility that emit red or NIR light with high quantum efficiency is also the focus of our research.
(2)
Most research has focused on the design and application of Ch-CPDs. Few researchers have considered the mechanisms of chirality and chiral reconstruction. There remains no satisfactory explanation for the chiral mechanism of CPDs, and the lack of clear understanding will prevent the full development and utilization of Ch-CPDs. Revealing the mechanism and rules of chiral generation, transmission, amplification, and regulation, and clarifying the structure–function relationship of Ch-CPDs are current research challenges.
(3)
The chiral assembly of CPDs is at an early stage, and the chiral matrix materials used in assembly are very limited. The design and assembly of Ch-CPDs-based composite materials faces similar problems. The unique photoelectric properties of Ch-CPDs and their easy functionalization provide the basis for forming composites with other materials. Such composites will possess the complementary advantages of the different components, resulting in qualitative improvements in their properties and application. The chiral assembly of CPDs and Ch-CPDs-based composites represents both a challenge and an opportunity.

Ch-CPDs are a new class of chiral nanomaterials that have potential applications in many fields due to their outstanding optical and electronic properties. However, there remain many areas for development. We hope that this review, by collecting existing information on Ch-CPDs, will draw more research attention and inspire new ideas.

Yi Ru: Writing - original draft. Lin Ai: Writing - review & editing. Tongtong Jia: Investigation. Xingjiang Liu: Investigation. Siyu Lu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Zhiyong Tang: Investigation. Bai Yang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yi Ru is currently a M.S. student of College of Chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Siyu Lu at Zhengzhou University. Her major research focuses on chiral carbon dots and chiral perovskite.

Lin Ai is currently a post-doctor of College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. She received her Ph.D. degree in polymer physics and chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Hao Zhang at Jilin University. Her research interests relate to the novel fluorescent nanomaterials, mainly focus on polymer carbon dots and metal nanoclusters. The research work includes fluorescence mechanism exploration and further photoelectric application.

Tongtong Jia obtained his B. S. in the College of Chemistry from Zhengzhou University in 2019. He is currently a M.S. student in the Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He is presently working on photocatalytic hydrodehalogenation and photoredox catalysis.

Siyu Lu is currently an associate professor of College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. He received his Ph.D. degree in polymer physics and chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Bai Yang at Jilin University. His research interests relate to the design, preparation, properties of photoelectric nanocrystals (Carbon dots, Semiconductors, etc.) and their applications in fluorescence and catalysis.

Bai Yang is currently a professor of the State Key Lab of Supramolecular Structure and Materials in the College of Chemistry, Jilin University. He received his Ph.D. in polymer chemistry and physics in 1991 under the supervision of Prof. Jiacong Shen at Jilin University. His research interests relate to the polymeric carbon nanodots: syntheses, nanostructure and properties, polymeric nanohybridization and photoelectronic materials and devices via aqueous approaches, high-performance polymeric hybrid optical materials as well as ordered fabrication of polymeric microstructures with multiscale and photonic responsive materials.
